author: Submarine_Man
text: Attached is a document that examines the various repeater parameters (rxdelay, txdelay, direct.txdelay, CR) and proposes a density metric to automatically adjust them to optimize a repeater for its current environment. By automating this, the entire mesh would improve (collisions would be reduced) and repeater owners that leave their repeaters at the non-optimum default values would have them automatically adjusted to an improved value. Comments for improvements and corrections would be greatly appreciated.

author: Skye
text: Very goood research you should post it in github discusssions as well https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/discussions

author: clauwn - BSmesh.de
text: The paper indicates you did some testing on actual hardware, what was your testing setup?

author: Submarine_Man
text: I’m not sure what you are referring to.

author: clauwn - BSmesh.de
text: maybe i misunderstood. did you test the proposed values on hardware?

author: Submarine_Man
text: We manually set our repeaters with the values, however the true benefit is achieved when the neighbor repeaters use them. Setting our repeaters benefitted our neighbors, by spreading our transmissions and reducing or eliminating a collision from us. We only used the values with two local repeaters, but with several other repeaters around, which did not use them, we could not make a definitive assessment. (edited)

author: clauwn - BSmesh.de
text: i can try and get this tested in a bigger mesh, how could i validate success?

author: Submarine_Man
text: Well I would choose a dense mesh (this should be having collisions). Baseline the stats of the repeaters (the ones of interest are the uptime, TX/RX packets and error packets) of each and record the current settings. Manually count the neighbors and find the setting and set them via the CLI. Let it run for a week and recollect the stats. Hopefully the error rate has gone down. I’m not sure how to measure if the Delivered / Failed DM rate changes (are ACKs getting through better).

author: Submarine_Man
text: Then set the values back to the original run for a week and compare old vs. new RX rate, TX rate, Error rate.

author: Submarine_Man
text: Section 7, CR is mostly an overview on how it works, but does have the four paragraphs for the 4 density environments. The “beef” is in section 9.4.2/2 which added a CR column and the algorithm is used to choose the row. There is a note regarding Sparse, it may be easier to just go one notch higher, rather than tweaking the counting to include low SNR neighbors (it’s not much of a hit to airtime) and simplifies how it’s measured.

text: 1.14 has identified that it is non-trivial to get everyone to update all of the repeaters. While I’m absolutely in favor of adding this as a dynamic function within the firmware, it’s going to take awhile before it sees adoption. More so with an entirely different fork of the firmware.

author: Submarine_Man
text: I totally agree, but I believe the easiest way to mass adoption is to just put it in the code. If they update, they get the automatic optimization benefit, if they don't it's the same as what we have today. Over time we will catch everyone. I also agree that some sort of testing could be done, but as you noted, it's not easy to get a group of people to participate and to really validate this it needs to be tried on the various "mesh environments". Maybe just a theoretical assessment is all that is required. I believe the new settings can only improve the current situation (given that the defaults are way too low). If you have "any results" with your static manual setting please let me know, I would like to see what happened. I've been thinking I might need to buy a nice waterfall frequency analyzer, with storage, to capture what is happening at my roof, I suspect that collisions are occurring, but I'm not sure what type of instrument can capture the RF coming in and identify what is going on...

author: paul / BostonMesh

author: Aleksey M.
text: a pull request has appeared that implements the described autotuning logic https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/2125/

author: Aleksey M.
text: A not very expensive SDR receiver with a good receiving path (bandpass filter) is enough to see collisions at the waterfall in a fairly dense network. I see them all the time. In addition, I think that on the attached SNR graph of all messages received by my companion, this "hanging down fringe" is actually collisions that sharply reduce the SNR of received packets. There is not much other interference in our band. So I am very glad to see your autotuning proposal, thank you very much! Unfortunately, I'm sure that I won't be able to get a significant number of neighboring repeaters to update to the test build, so I'm looking forward to the official release of this amazing feature. (edited)

author: Submarine_Man
text: Who makes the SDR you have? I presume a good Cavity Filter in between the antenna and SDR would work well to keep out any surrounding noise. It also looks like there is MeshCore software that is decoding the ID of the messages that it’s receiving, where do it get that? Thanks! for the tip…

author: Aleksey M.
text: Nothing special, just SDRplay RSPdx R2 with an inexpensive Chinese SAW-filter. And I collect statistics on received messages using a USB companion and HomeAssistant with meshcore integration and automation that creates entities for the last prefixes from the packet path, as well as placing the corresponding SNR and RSSI in them. This data is accumulated with InfluxDB and visualized using Grafana. This looks quite complicated to someone unfamiliar with HomeAssistant and related solutions, but it allows you to get a useful result in a shorter time compared to developing a special application. Here is the link to the HomeAssistant integration site https://meshcore-dev.github.io/meshcore-ha/ And a link to a solution that saves the SNR of packets received from repeaters https://github.com/WJ4IoT/Meshcore-Home-Assistant-Solutions/blob/main/SNR_Repeater.md (edited)

author: bluebrolly.
text: Currently there is a global on or off for the buzzer but that doesn’t give enough control over buzzer notifications. The public channel will have a lot of traffic and potentially useless messages, the buzzer will get annoying and you will want to have a buzzer triggered only for messages for a specific important channel or multiple channels. See attached video. I implemented in device firmware the ability to disable and enable notifications for each channel. The global buzzer_quiet is still there and is used as the primary flag. If buzzer is enabled then the per channel flag bits are used. The mobile app you see that looks new is my development app I use to test new settings that are not currently available in the main public android app. In the future I think we should have custom tones or melodies that we can assign per channel so when we aren’t looking at our phone or screen we can at least know via audio a message came in on which channel. I’ll post this on GitHub once I do a bit more testing and make sure it doesn’t cause any issues anywhere else

author: emi
text: https://github.com/kore-signet/chiyocore Working on a custom, from-scratch implementation of meshcore firmware, in Rust, for ESP32 boards. nifty features: supports most of the meshcore companion protocol, so you can use the standard apps supports multiple nodes with different setups (combinations of companion + repeater + bot + observer + etc) on one board (repeater + observer + more complex bots are all todos; currently you can just run several companions and ping bots at once lol) (this is not meant to be for mobile repeaters at all) all configuration of these setups is done in a single human readable config file! works on multicore ESP32s using both cores at once very speedy (hardware accelerated cryptography) fun project :) still very much a wip! it is extremely untested , currently only really works on ESP32-S3s, and I have only actually run it on my XiaoS3 kit from Seeed. other boards are still a todo.

author: Zaos
text: Companion relay 1.Relays public messages by zero hop repeating any flood messages it has heard 2.Relays Messages with specific companions as destination by zero hop repeating it. 3.Relays messages from specific companions to public flood by normal flood repeating it. 4.Relays messages from specific companions to its next path dest by flood. Use-case 1.Consistency of conversion - By having 1 companion you'll be able to see your entire convo history. you dont need to flip flop between multiple devices to see what's happening. 2.Range extension - By having a relay on the car, you can have it relay messages to your client thats in a much worse position such as in an office building or a store. This is without messing with peoples routings and reducing the airtime compared to if it was just a repeater. Why not Repeater? -Repeaters are meant to be stationary, too many moving repeaters cause extra traffic and messes with routing, while it is predicted to not cause a real issue on message reliability, it can still cause UX issues as message retries and flood to find a new path is timely. Why not Companion? -Companions dont share their messages, so if you have a companion you use daily, then get into your car, now you wont be able to message outside of the car efficiently. if you put a node ontop of your car, now you have to swap your app to your car companion, which breaks up conversations and ruins message history since now you wont be seeing previous messages, DM's now must also swap. i.e if "Jason" in this situation is messaging my client - "Zaos" and sends me a DM, but i actually have my zaos client in my pocket or left it at home since Im inside of my car driving, well now that message could entirely be lost, or i just wont see it since my car client is called "Zaos-Mobile" and its a different ID entirely. It is possible to shared the same key but i have not dived into that and it does not seem ideal security wise. (edited)

author: stretch ZL3XL
text: @Zaos people are saying "You're building MT" because what you're describing sounds awfully like MT's new "Zero-cost hops" feature. https://meshtastic.org/blog/zero-cost-hops-favorite-routers/ If your new relay role listens for messages FROM your companion only, and zero-hop repeats them, I guess no one beyond your companion and your relay are any the wiser. If your relay listens for messages TO your companion, and forwards them, and ignores any other messages, then it is indeed a relay and not a repeater.

author: Zeb Palmer (IdahoMesh)
text: Love the work you're doing here, it's very much needed. But please think seriously about how to name it. "Companion Relay"... I think that's going to confuse a lot of folks. Considering the primary use case for this behavior on MC is in a mobile environment, perhaps "Mobile Relay?" I think one of the fatal flaws of MT has been the prolifereation of roles and the fact that most people don't understand them. I love the simplicity of MC having two simple ones, but I very much believe we need something for this use case--with a good name. And I would very much focus on pitching this as a way to reduce the impact of mobile repeaters that already exist rather than adding a new role that will take up more air time. (edited)

author: bp-gone
text: I haven't thought this all the way through, and it may not solve the "mobile repeater" problems... but avoiding adding new commands, could you just: 1) For FLOOD_GROUP_TEXT, if the path length is zero (first hop), act as a repeater (repeat as normal) 2) For FLOOD_GROUP_TEXT, if the path length is > 0, repeat it with zero hops. In #1, that packet could be from your companion in H-E-B, or it could be someone else nearby, and you aren't really hurting the mesh. Maybe even less so if your txdelay is set high (you are adding a hop and adding delay, so other repeaters nearby will ignore it?) Not sure of the other types of packets you might apply the same logic to...

author: Zaos
text: reduced airtime for people who already run/want to run repeaters on their cars

author: bluebrolly.
text: Oh ok.

author: Zaos
text: Companion relay 1.Relays public messages by zero hop repeating any flood messages it has heard 2.Relays Messages with specific companions as destination by zero hop repeating it. 3.Relays messages from specific companions to public flood by normal flood repeating it. 4.Relays messages from specific companions to its next path dest by flood. Use-case 1.Consistency of conversion - By having 1 companion you'll be able to see your entire convo history. you dont need to flip flop between multiple devices to see what's happening. 2.Range extension - By having a relay on the car, you can have it relay messages to your client thats in a much worse position such as in an office building or a store. This is without messing with peoples routings and reducing the airtime compared to if it was just a repeater. Why not Repeater? -Repeaters are meant to be stationary, too many moving repeaters cause extra traffic and messes with routing, while it is predicted to not cause a real issue on message reliability, it can still cause UX issues as message retries and flood to find a new path is timely. Why not Companion? -Companions dont share their messages, so if you have a companion you use daily, then get into your car, now you wont be able to message outside of the car efficiently. if you put a node ontop of your car, now you have to swap your app to your car companion, which breaks up conversations and ruins message history since now you wont be seeing previous messages, DM's now must also swap. i.e if "Jason" in this situation is messaging my client - "Zaos" and sends me a DM, but i actually have my zaos client in my pocket or left it at home since Im inside of my car driving, well now that message could entirely be lost, or i just wont see it since my car client is called "Zaos-Mobile" and its a different ID entirely. It is possible to shared the same key but i have not dived into that and it does not seem ideal security wise.^ (edited)

author: Gilles
text: I have compiled all the firmware roles for the 1.14.1 on the C6L. I did it with the help of an agent and it is a basically the changes made by TheRealHaoLiu merged into the latest version of MeshCore. I'm not a developper so I prefer not to publish the source on Github but I'll be happy to share them in private while they are relevant. I build the following roles: Repeater Room Server Companion BLE Companion USB Sensor Terminal Chat They can be flashed on the unit using the following command: esptool --chip esp32c6 -p /dev/PORT erase-flash esptool --chip esp32c6 -p /dev/PORT write-flash --flash-mode dio --flash-freq 80m --flash-size 4MB 0x0 m5stack_unit_c6l_ROLE-merged.bin This is the only command that worked for me without entering into that annoying boot loop. (edited)

author: bluebrolly.
text: I tested 2.4GHz LoRA for LoRAWAN and the range is not great. you have issues penetrating through walls, 2.4Ghz noise, microwaves and water still reduce your signal. The LoRA modulation helps cut through some of the 2.4GHz noise that already exists, but unless you are outdoors, line of sight and good weather, your range is not great and kind of defeats the purpose. If ANYTHING for a medium range solution, I would of recommended WiFi HALOW instead of LoRA as an alternative medium range solution that gives you a lot more speed and can do voice, audio and TCP/IP. (edited)

author: Viper7777
text: I agree that Morse Micro’s Wifi Halow is better because of the security ect. Why I would still vote for the Base Duo is, that it is already developed for MT and ready to go, it has a $40 price tag. The Wifi Halow is not ready and will cost a lot more if I look at their products they already have. For just communication, like what MT and MC offers, it still think the Muzi Works Base Duo is worth the effort of getting it on MC.

author: bluebrolly.
text: When you hard disconnect power , there is no battery connected right ? How long do you keep it unplugged before you plug it back in?

author: Lbibass
text: Maybe 30 seconds?

author: bluebrolly.
text: That’s long enough for capacitors to drain. If you pull power , short vcc and ground, make sure no battery is plugged in. That will remove any juice in the capacitors. It might be a timing issue also with how the display is initialized. Meaning there needs to be more of a delay for the screen to power on and communicate.

author: Lbibass
text: I can try and give it like... a one second delay or something? I'm not sure where the display initialization is taking place

author: bluebrolly.
text: I don’t have this device so I can’t help too much. A theory is that the module is booting up faster than the screen, it tries to unit and gets no ack over i2c. A reset button press will only trigger a reboot of the module but your screen is staying powered. When you push the reset, you trigger a new init of the display

text: OK. Just got my order for the RAK 1W. Let's see how this adventure goes.

author: ThatGuy
text: So far, I'm finding the 1W is much more susceptible to background noise. I'm going to order a different solar management board and see if that helps my situation. I deployed my 1W to a site on Friday where I had been getting good service from a Rak4631 node, and it performed horribly. To the point of being unusable and unreachable. Literally swapped out the boards, and added the power pins for the 5v input to the header pins on my power board, and no other changes. Same battery, same solar board, same antenna. Ended up going back at the end of the day to put the 4631 back in the so I would have connectivity during the ice storm. The .250W board went right back to working great. Getting 16.5 mile connectivity tall building to tower range. Couldn't see the tower at all with the 1W board. I'm underimpressed so far. (edited)

This is a test location for us right now. The node will eventually be mounted about 20 feet up on a sign structure that is away from most of that electrical gear. I was really surprised at the performance drop though. I didn't expect that at all. If you don't mind, can you share your power setup for the 1W?

I’m also running an observer and seeing that the bot successfully TX’d but the booster did not repeat it back out the mesh at large.

author: andyshinn
text: it definitely has more options for powering and other variables. for troubleshooting i think it is helpful to know the radio power path, antenna pigtail, antenna, etc. just based on previous experience alone on the higher power radios i would estimate that the 13302 is slightly more deaf than the 4631 (which is the "gold standard" at receiving in my experience). so, more messages may be getting to their destination. but the return ack may be more unreliable i've got mine now running for more than 48 hours on the ground after swapping out to a waveshare solar board since it claims 3A on the 5V output where most others only seem to be 1A. the RAK docs call out needing 1.5A on EX_5V. so, i imagine there are going to be power concerns that people run into a bunch

author: ThatGuy
text: Sure. I initially was using the DFRobot 5v Solar manager boards, but couldn't get stable results. Further research after I bought several of them indicate they have a reputation for being noisy. So, I ordered a Waveshare Solar Power Manager (D) board, hooked up to the RAK3401 via USB-C and out of the 5v green port to the 5v-EXT, with the jumper set to external power. It worked great at home. At home, it had a ALFA 915 5dbi antenna directly mounted on the bulkhead adapter on the top of the box. When I deployed it downtown, nothing changed on the inside of the box, other than hooking up the 12v solar panel to the waveshare board. Externally, I went with a Rokland 5.8dbi antenna (which was on my 4631 test node downtown), connected via a 3' piece of premade RFC-400 low loss with appropriate connectors which I also got from Rokland. I'm waiting on a second Waveshare board to be delivered to my house so I can put it on the other 1W that's there so I can eliminate that problem from the analysis loop. (edited)

author: andyshinn
text: the dfrobot board is also only 1A output max, correct? it's possible that it spikes higher. but i also wonder if in that case it's dropping voltage a lot and the PA isn't getting what it needs. on the voltaic MPPT board i had it was rated for 1A but it was dropping enough that the MCU eventually didn't recover from a brownout the waveshare is 3A. though, i haven't any real way to verify their claims... it would be ncie to get some real measurements from some of these solar boards and the RAK 1W at it's most power hungry spikes

author: Eddie
text: I just received my kit and so far its been disappointing! I have it powered by USB with the same antenna as my other devices (other devices have batteries from their OEM). Bluetooth seems to drop just a few feet or even inches away. This happens on both of my Android phones (Pixel 9 Pro daily driver) and I plan on resurrecting a third phone that is in the box for ewaste recycling since that would be an older BLE standard. I can leave my other devices upstairs in the window, go downstairs below them and no BLE issues. Based on the absence of a message saying BLE has connected, it seems that it only sees a FRACTION of the packets that the ThinkNode M1 sees. But there's more... those missing packets in the RX log are not just from BLE dropping...the LORA side of the house is only seeing fraction of the packets since I see only a fraction of the messages in the public channel. My initial testing was on 1.13 and I upgraded my other devices from 1.12 to 1.13 to try to make it as scientific as possible. I downgraded to 1.12 (full erase) and the results are the same. I don't have any temp sensors, but its been cool to the touch so far. I am considering trying MeshTastic to see if it has the issues I have seen to see if it is bad hardware, but my experience with MT was so abysmal I hate that do that. 8-)

author: ThatGuy
text: @ccw I'm not an expert, and there are several simultaneous issues going on here, so it can be kind of confusing. I'll explain my understanding of it, but there's a fair chance I don't fully understand. For a while, people have been observing that the RAK 1W boards, particularly in noisy RF environments, are "going deaf". Speculation on this has focus around a couple of different areas. 1) the automatic gain controller essentially dialing up the gain until the receiver overloads and can't hear anything through the noise, and then not properly resetting when the noise floor decreases. The AGC is supposed to adjust itself automatically. In order to force the board to reset it, people have been trying 'set agc.reset.interval ' to make the board reset itself every _ seconds (It has to be a multiple of 4). Some people have seen performance increases by doing so. I am one of them. It wasn't huge, but it was noticable. 2) There is also speculation that since the 13302 board has its own reception booster and filter on board, that the built in software RX Boost, which is turned on in the MC firmware by default it essentially causing a "double boost", and making the receiver go deaf. Some people have also seen performance increases by disabling the software RX Boost. Initially, you had to do this by compiling an custom firmware to turn it off, b/c the setting was housed in a compile time build flag. Somone created a PR that makes it user selectable in the repeater command line interface via the ' {get/set} radio.lna {on/off}' command. Then, yesterday, @weebl2000 discovered that the FW may not have had all of the necessary configuration for the 13302 board to be correctly powered and used, which may have been contributing to some of the issues. He patched the code to fix that. Some people also experience overheating issues with the 1W boards. Could be software problem. Might be defective hardware. (edited)

author: ccw

author: Aenigma
text: Hello all - new member here and new Meshcore user. I have one of these 1W RAK kits on order so I’m following along closely. I wanted to commend you for your determination to optimize this firmware. One quick question: what SX1262 power out level setting are you using? Reading the SKY66122-11 datasheet, a max input power level is +16dBm. I know the SX1262 can go up to +22dBm so I was curious how that aspect was being handled. The typical insertion loss of the SAW filter is about 2dB so I would think any SX1262 output power setting over 18dB might start over-driving the PA. Also, on the RX side for the SX1262, I don’t think the boosted gain setting is necessary seeing that the SKY66122-11 has 16dB of gain. Boosting in the SX1262 will just amplify the noise seeing the SKY66122-11 has just an OK noise figure. Anyway, not sure if this is helpful but wanted to say thanks again for what you are doing! (edited)

author: Aenigma
text: I guess I have to question https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1836 CSD and CPS on the SKY66122 are both connected to WisBlock IO3 per https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/wisblock/RAK13302/datasheet/ Setting IO3 to logic high would take it from shutdown/sleep to enabling the LNA on the SKY66122. Also, CTX is connected directly to the DIO2 pin on the SX1262, assuming that the SX1262 has been setup with “SetDIO2AsRfSwitchCtrl” in section 11.3 of the SX1262 datasheet. Similarly, the circuit is setup such that the SX1262 controls the TCXO via its DIO3 which would require “SetDIO3AsTcxoCtrl”. Finally, the boost regulator, U5, is controlled by WisBlock IO2 and only necessary if a battery is being used (jumper set to IN_5V) versus directly powering the RAK13302 via the 5V connection on the bottom and setting the jumper to EX_5V. My thoughts/theory is that maybe the control I/Os aren’t being properly handled per the circuit intention/implementation. If the CSD/CPS (IO3) is going low after TX for whatever reason, then the SKY66122 is effectively being turned off and that may explain the unit going “deaf”. (edited)

author: Aenigma
text: Hopefully that all makes sense. I read the comments in the PR regarding the control pins and it didn’t match what was shown on the schematics.

author: Aenigma
text: All of my comments above rely on https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/wisblock/RAK13302/datasheet/ being accurate but I am seeing discrepancies between the schematics and the connector diagram. For example, EX_5V (via Schottky D1) should not be connected to USB+ (Pin 7) on the board-to-board connector and have it power the RAK3401 properly - it should be connected to VBUS (Pin 9). The whole documentation is a bit of a mess from RAK…

author: Aenigma
text: One last thought then I’m done for the day: how are folks powering this kit? Could the boost converter (if you are using the IN_5V path with a battery connected, charging via USB) be causing extra noise to be present in the circuit, raising the noise floor? Has anyone done an A-B test between IN_5V and EX_5V (no battery connected)? Huh I just looked closer and EN on the boost converter (U5) is NC to the IO2 connection on the WisBlock so it would always be enabled if a battery is connected.

text: I am concerned that, if the EX_5V is wired up to USB+ like it shows on the schematics, it could cause some issues with the RAK3401 module and may be a cause of the overheating. The device could be getting back-powered through ESD diodes or some other path that’s not intentional (should be VBUS). If overheating happens when using EX_5V but not when using a battery+USB input (IN_5V jumper setting), that could explain things. I have a support question out to RAK questioning their schematics and if it is indeed how the RAK13302 module is wired. (edited)

text: I'd be interested to see what you find. I don't know anything about that particular filter. I'd suggest testing by refreshing repeater stats, and recording the RSSI, Noise Floor and SNR. Perhaps do them side by side, one with filter and one without, and then compare. Try to keep other hardware like antennas, power, etc. the same.

author: ccw
text: The 1W raks have a filter built in already

author: curtisc83
text: Are the filters good?

author: ccw
text: Like you, I haven’t checked exhaustively, but as best I can tell, not bad. There’s the issue I was mentioning, namely that it’ll adjust the amplifier gain to keep the noise floor around -105 pretty much independent of what’s going on around you. If I drive up to a mystery transmitter that takes my V3 up to like -80 or worse and hold it there, the rak will get a momentary spike and go back to baseline and then a negative spike when I drive away, so there’s nothing to compare to directly without more effort, but my sense is that of the radios I have, the rak hears best

author: curtisc83
text: I live in a city would a ceramic filter be beneficial since there is more RF floating around. From what you wrote I basically have two filters on my 1 watt raks now. I’m wondering is there a downside?

author: ccw
text: For an authoritative answer you want either an expert on these radios or experiment, but in principle, sure: among the downsides filters aren’t perfect, so every layer you add is throwing away signal too and costing transmit performance. My guess is if you try the built in filter and the background noise is still bad enough to cause you problems, you’d want to try a different strategy than just stacking up more filters of similar performance.

author: ThatGuy
text: I think almost everything we worked on on the 3401 has been merged. It's probably safe to go to the nightlies at this point.

author: curtisc83

author: ccw
text: Sweet

author: ccw
text: I defer to the experts, and there are no doubt exceptions, but as a rule, no. Especially not if with every layer you’re adding connectors and cable and so forth. But no worries, because cheap as they are, the ceramic filters aren’t free, and for the price of a stack of them, you can just buy a cavity filter

author: ThatGuy
text: Since we have a quality group of testers in this thread, could I get y'all's help on testing this PR? It's to put a companion into OTA mode remotely for OTA updates, so you could use the Nordic app (other others) to update the firmware over BLE, without having to physically hit the button or plug up a cable. I think I have it working. I've tested on the RAK 4631 and the 3401, but I'd like to make sure the results are reproducable. Ultimately, there will be a button for it in the MC app. Until then, there is a python script that will trigger it. If you get a chance, try it out and let me know if it works for you. https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/2078 (edited)

author: ThatGuy
text: I'm not convinced that we have the transmit side of things working quite right on the 3401 boards. Is anyone else experiencing less than expected transmit performance? For example, we put a node up this weekend with 3401, powered by a solar panel that has 26000 mAh of battery, with a 12v to 5v converter that puts out up to 10A of power. Running to the 5v_EXT port with jumper set accordingly. So, power shouldn't be an issue. It's running a Diamond BC920 antenna. It's mouted at 190 feet up a tower. There was a 4631-based node about 90 feet lower on the tower, with a similar (if slightly inferior) antenna. I adverted from both and then looked at my next highest node that can hear that area. The 4631 advert was received at +4.25 dB. The 3401 was -4.50dB . Repeated and got the same results. Reception on the 3401 is outstanding however. Connect quality to the new node is a lot less reliable than the 4631-based node was. Anyone have and thoughts/guesses? (edited)

author: Aenigma
text: How does the Meshcore TX power setting compare to the actual SX1262 TX output power as an input to the SKY66122 amp?

author: Aenigma
text: I looked more closely at the schematics - there is a Pi attenuator between the SX1262 and the SKY66122 of about -20.6dB. When you add in the insertion loss of the SAW filter, setting the SX1262 to +22dBm puts about 0dBm into the SKY66122. Since the S21 (small signal gain) is about 33dB, that should get you up to the max +30dBm (1W) at the output at 5V. (edited)

author: weebl2000
text: Yeah meshcore just tells the SX1262 how much power to output

author: Aenigma

author: ccw
text: So just to toss out a possibility: might be two problems here, one a consequence of the other. I’ve had one of these boards behaving very well for weeks on weebl’s variant 4 way up the thread, no overheating, apparently infinite battery life. Left it on the porch the other day and it got hot out there, like 130F air temp and idk how hot the board got. It locked up and was still >140 when it became responsive again. Lost a bunch of the nonvolatile memory contents, or at least its record of what was stored where. Anywho, point being: if there’s a bug overheating the MCU, lockups and other misbehavior may occur due to the heat itself

text: Per the RAK information, the battery must be attached if you are using the USB port and have the jumper set to internal 5 V. The battery gets boosted to 5 V on the radio module, and the USB alone will not power the radio module.

text: For your broken board, does the red charge light ever come on? Or was that the one where it would only be a dim red light? I suspect the charger IC is the problem. It’s not properly sourcing power from USB and delivering it to the battery terminals. That would explain why it would show 0% versus the one that does work showing 100% when no battery is attached. (edited)

author: Not Sure
text: The "22" max setting we see in software controls the SX1262 chip. A separate (but integrated in the package) amplifier bumps it to 30dBm in the 1W. So there shouldn't be any way overdrive it by software unless the 1262 has a fault. The same applies to 22 being 28dBm out on the Heltec v4. Think of ~22 as a scaling factor not a set number as it was before higher power chips came along.

author: 👽 wazmac
text: I have just been testing with a local contact in Sydney and we have some interesting observations. We have clear LoS over 31km and a pair of RAK 1W repeaters. Between these two nodes we are getting consistent birdirectional SNR of 10.5db to 11db with relatively high noise floors of 84 and 94. The interesting thing though is that they do not seem not outstanding at all when communicating with other nodes. (edited)

author: siliex
text: fwiw, aside from accidentally burning out the radio (which was user error, not the board's fault) I've only had good experiences with this board so far - just got my replacement today and put it in, working great. Both boards I've run have had way better performance than the 4631 I was using in the exact same spot, though they also had a higher noise floor which is I guess expected. Weird how variable people's experiences are with this thing.

author: ThatGuy
text: For what it is worth, I was finally able to lay my hands on a TinySA to test one of my RAK 1W kits that wasn't working as expected. As I suspected, it's a burned out transmitter section. Tried it on multiple versions of the firmware, and in all possible power configs. Maximum transmit I could get out of it was -35 db. I compared it with a new-in-package 1W, which showed +29.5 db or so using the same testing setup and powered solely through the EXT 5V connector with appropriate jumper selection. While I was at it, I went ahead and tested the new working board with the jumper set to INT 5V, and using only battery and then only USB, and it had underwhelming transmit performance on both...in the range of -10db. Powering with both Battery and USB transmitted at +29.4 db or so, as designed. So, they aren't kidding about needing to use both sources simultaneously if you're powering from the main board and not using the EXT_5V connector. If you decide to replicate this testing yourself, please note that you **__need ** a 40db or so signal attenuator to avoid burning out the front end on the TinySA, which is rated apparently up to about 10db. 1W = roughly 30db, so the 40 attenuator gives you plenty of room. You just have to do the math at the end. (edited)

author: ThatGuy
text: TinySA setup: I used a TinySA Ultra+. Calibrate the unit according to the manufacturer’s instructions. After this, hook up as shown below. Tap the Screen. Hit Frequency. I set up the frequency range on the device to scan from 910.40 to 911.60, since I use 910.525 as my center frequency. This keeps the scanning path short and makes it less likely you’ll miss the transmission peak. Back out to the main menu, then Tap “Trace”. If it isn’t already selected, pick a trace you want to use. I used Trace 1. Click “Enable”. Then click “Calc Off”. Pick “Max Hold”. This will cause it to lock the highest peak on the screen so you can read it. After you’ve taken a measurement, and want to do another, tap the screen again, and hit “Max Hold” again. This will reset the pointer peak. You can do this over and over as you take measurements.

author: ThatGuy
text: I was told a VNA wouldn't do it. Has to be a SA. I have a NanoVNA H4 as well, but a couple of folks on here, plus AI, both indicated a TinySA was the way to go. A friend got one, and I borrowed it.

author: ThatGuy
text: Yes, at least as far as I can tell. It seems RAK has changed the wording on the docs yet again , but previously, it said you had to do either 5V EXT for everything, or Battery + USB or Battery + a 5v source into the Solar Port. I don't understand why 5v into the USB port wouldn't do it alone, but earlier versions of the docs said it wouldn't, and my testing supports that. I plugged up USB, forgot the battery for a moment, and switched it over to INT, and was getting lower than expected transmit. Then, I was like, 'oh yeah, the battery', plugged it in, and it jumped up to 30db immediately. I don't have the known good board here anymore. I sent it out for deployment today, so I can't reverify immediately. Once I lay hands back on a working 1W, I'll verify this result again.

author: Aenigma
text: For those wondering about the power paths, if you look at the schematics, the SKY66122 FEM only gets powered from either the EX_5V input OR the 5V boost converter which takes the battery voltage as its input. The USB VBUS is NOT connected to the amp, that’s why it won’t work properly with just USB input. (edited)

author: Codes
text: I haven't looked at the schematic, but from what I understand, the board doesn't do any USB-PD triggering to allow the supply to provide more amps or volts on the USB. The 190007 can only pull 450ma from USB to charge the battery, so 450ma on the battery rail is just not enough to run the 5v booster

author: ThatGuy

author: awhite
text: Recently we've been seeing something that looks like payload corruption in packets, but with headers intact. This causes packets to be needlessly forwarded and creates "monster paths" - see https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/1386 for more details. Such partially corrupted packets could be dropped early if the protocol added a field in the header (or a new trailer) to pass a few ECC bits calculated over the whole packet.

author: awhite
text: another point to check and quickly discard corrupted packets - in the ticket above we've observed packets with path_len=132. This should be immediately detected as a value above MAX_PATH_LEN and the packet discarded.

author: Beylix
text: Lora does already have error correction over the transmission by itself already by using CR right? Maybe we need to stick with CR8 instead of CR5 for better error correction

author: awhite
text: we’re not sure how the errors got in, maybe it’s a custom buggy rpt or a node with a brownout - the packets are almost the same but random bits are flipped.

author: Mike WB6KTN
text: Circling back to this after looking at Lora.. The header is fixed size and sent at a code rate of 8. The header tells the receiver enough to decode the payload. Extending the header would be a massive undertaking as it needs to be a multiple of the symbol size and ... It is normal for a short header with high FEC to be received correctly while a longer payload with low FEC to fail. Though, I would have expected the payload to have failed recovery and been discarded.

author: awhite
text: ah, maybe I didn't communicate this properly .. I didn't think the errors came from the LoRa layer, rather it looks like they were introduced at the software layer (mem corruption? brownout? buggy custom rpt?). So the LoRa data was correct but the MC packet data was slightly incorrect - the payload is checksummed and it was valid, but the MC header was not and it's not checksummed.

author: Mike WB6KTN
text: Seems I misunderstood. There is no protection for data corrupted before or after LoRa gets it. LoRa separately protects the header and the payload. Are you suggesting some sort of application level protection (checksum) over the entire packet? On a perhaps related topic, yes, packets that have unreasonable values should be discarded.

author: awhite
text: I'm a belt + suspenders type of guy we should definitely verify that packets have valid and/or sane values.

author: BerndFfm
text: I would like to enter an interval the adverts with gps data is send, e.g. every 5 minutes and 12 h if not moving. This should work without connected to an app. We will use this in search and rescue missions to locate vehicles, drones and mission forces in regions where there is no internet and mobile network. We also will use this in operations in other countries after earthquakes and natural disasters where there is no infrastructure. This feature can also be used at large concerts, on hiking tours and anywhere there is poor cell phone coverage. Also when working in the forest. It would be good to specify a scope like #sarmission for a very small area, so the MeshCore will not be bothered more than necessary. So this can be done with the following fields : [X] Live Tracking Intervall : 12 h Intervall when moving : 5 min Moving distance : 100 m Scope : sarmission Without scope this option should not work or scope is set to 'local'. Alternatively : Query GPS data from a device. The data is queried by the internal GPS receiver before sending, i.e. always the current position. Or you have a field how often gps is updated by the firmware e.g. 5 min. (edited)

author: Hugo
text: Something like Meshcore TEAM's "Autonomous Mode"? https://github.com/tmacinc/MeshCore-TEAM#how-autonomous-mode-works

author: BerndFfm
text: Yes, exactly like MeshCore TEAM or MeshCore SAR, but without a phone connected to the device. In a mission we give the T1000 Cards to people that don't have a special app on the phone. Our SAR dogs also will get a T1000 Card, they don't have a phone.

author: Hugo
text: My understanding is that the Autonomous Mode for TEAM is exactly that: It requires their custom firmware, but specifically does work without a phone app connected. You: Load the custom firmware Use TEAM to set autonomous mode and set the GPS interval (time) or distance traveled threshold Periodic and distance-triggered GPS updates will then continue from the radio completely independently, even with a phone/app not connected From the linked section on Autonomous Mode specifically. (edited)

author: Christopher A. Wichura
text: You can also configure the node to allow telemetry requests. Then, instead of it burping periodically, you just ask it from a registered contact what its location is. The telemetry includes the current GPS reading. This is supported by mainline firmware today. (edited)

author: Kamiccolo
text: Made a fork on github, added separate action to build only E290 firmwares: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/compare/main...kamiccolo:MeshCore:main The result: https://github.com/kamiccolo/MeshCore/releases/tag/v1.14.0 So, if you have GitHub account, You could fork it for Your own use Basically it requires few things: synchronize with the upstream (one button click on web interface) create new tag (according to upstream, few more clicks) initiate build (a few more clicks) And after 7 minutes - fresh set of binaries

author: CyberDreamer
text: Is anybody working on implementing deepsleep firmware code for the heltec v4 so that it doesn't get stuck in a booting loop when voltage go under around 3.3v. Looked at the firmware code and it did seem top be part of other ESP32 boards, but while the heltec v4 has solar and battery management, no safety at all were implemented to prevent the board to drain the battery beyond 3.3v. Protected battery voltage cut off only kicking in under 2.9v for most of them.

author: FinMachJones
text: A firmware selector for the Heltec WiFi LoRa 32 V4.2 (ESP32-S3) that lets you switch between multiple firmwares at boot time — Meshtastic, MeshCore, Reticulum/RNode, or anything else. https://github.com/Finmacjones/HeltecV4.2MultiBoot

author: gjelsoe
text: You could do something like : ⁨ #define THERMISTOR_PIN 14 #define SERIES_RESISTOR 10000 // 10K ohm resistor #define THERMISTOR_NOMINAL 10000 // NTC resistance at 25°C #define TEMPERATURE_NOMINAL 25 // Temperature for nominal resistance #define B_COEFFICIENT 3380 // Beta coefficient for NCP18XH103F03RB #define ADC_MAX 4095 // 12-bit ADC #define VCC 3.3 // Supply voltage ⁩ And setting analog resolution : ⁨ analogReadResolution(12); // Set ADC to 12-bit resolution ⁩ Reading values and converting : ⁨ // Read ADC value (average of multiple readings for stability) float adcValue = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) { adcValue += analogRead(THERMISTOR_PIN); delay(10); } adcValue /= 10.0; // Convert ADC reading to voltage float voltage = (adcValue / ADC_MAX) * VCC; // Calculate thermistor resistance using voltage divider formula // For pull-up configuration: NTC is connected to VCC, resistor to GND // Vout = Vin * (R_series / (R_series + R_NTC)) // R_NTC = R_series * ((Vin / Vout) - 1) float resistance = SERIES_RESISTOR * ((VCC / voltage) - 1); // Calculate temperature using Steinhart-Hart simplified Beta equation // 1/T = 1/T0 + (1/B) * ln(R/R0) float steinhart; steinhart = resistance / THERMISTOR_NOMINAL; // (R/R0) steinhart = log(steinhart); // ln(R/R0) steinhart /= B_COEFFICIENT; // 1/B * ln(R/R0) steinhart += 1.0 / (TEMPERATURE_NOMINAL + 273.15); // + (1/T0) steinhart = 1.0 / steinhart; // Invert float temperatureC = steinhart - 273.15; // Convert to Celsius float temperatureF = temperatureC * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0; // Convert to Fahrenheit ⁩

author: griffc.
text: I've slopped up some changes and have got my board working again: Fixed progressive deafness — SX1262 register 0x8B5 patch now applied on init and after every AGC reset - AGC reset enabled by default — recalibrates the RX frontend every 60 seconds Proper LNA sequencing — RXEN pin now correctly managed around TX to prevent the LNA being left in a bad state Clean power cycle on boot — 50ms hard reset of the SX1262 module ensures a fresh start after soft reboot Will monitor. Re AGC reset - i have other TXs close by which might have turned the AGC down to such an extent it became totally deaf (edited)

author: pelgraine
text: Standalone/direct-device text entry for channel messages now available. Word wrap needs refining, but you now have the option of composing messages in Public or other channels using the device keyboard so aren't limited to the app alone. Channels other than Public will need to have been set with the phone app previously. There is currently a limit of 20 messages in the channel view message history. Please see the Readme for compose and channel navigation keyboard shortcuts and details: https://github.com/pelgraine/Meck/blob/main/README.md This is an early-stage build. UI improvements are definitely needed, but I would be thrilled with and welcome any feedback, info on bugs you've found or suggestions. https://github.com/pelgraine/Meck/releases/tag/text-entry-1

author: pelgraine

author: pelgraine
text: I haven't implemented phone calls just yet, so just SMS for now. Phone calls is something I'm still looking into but definitely hope to try and implement in future. If you hold down the user button (top one on the side) and then press the reset button briefly that should put it in DFU mode for flashing. Don't forget to format your SD card to FAT32. You can either use the Meshcore web flasher and choose the Custom Firmware option to flash the bin file- or if you wanted to have it alongside ripple on the Launcher, just copy the bin file to your SD card on your computer, then put the SD card back into your TD Pro. Then in Launcher mode on the Pro tap on the SD menu, tap the name of the firmware you want, and tap Install. If you haven't used Launcher much, fair warning that it'll say Install every time you want to swap between firmwares, regardless of whether you've installed that firmware previously. It's just a lot faster the second time around (edited)

author: pelgraine
text: Also don't forget that the Bluetooth is set to off by default once you flash/install it to save battery. Just press the user button or the d key a few times to scroll to the Bluetooth screen, and press the user button again or press enter to toggle it on. You'll then have to cycle back to the main screen to get the pin code (by pressing the user button or a key to cycle back - sorry, will fix that to be a more smooth experience in future)

author: pelgraine
text: I should also note I'm not a developer. Just someone trying to build something with the help of AI that's useful for myself, which others might also benefit from

author: pelgraine
text: I very much appreciate your reply, thank you, and sorry to hear you've had a frustrating experience with the Pro! These devices have so much potential that I hope more people start developing too. There is the standalone version without BLE for maximum battery life intended for use without a companion phone app, so if you tried that firmware it won't have worked with Bluetooth or the companion app at all - in the standalone version it shouldn't even display the Bluetooth page or pin at any point. The other versions of the firmware (4G and audio BLE) do, but as I mentioned Bluetooth is disabled on boot so after flashing or resetting the device you have to manually turn it on again. I also find when pairing with the iOS app for the first time it always gives you an error (regardless of firmware) so all I do is disconnect on the app and then tap the device in the pairing list in the app and it works the second go around. With the GPS I find the first time it can be a bit finicky unless you happen to be standing outside in open air, and even then can take around a minute or so to find sufficient satellites to get a fix. No map support at the moment, unfortunately, and I'm debating whether to implement that at all given Ripple does such an excellent job with map and GPS support. The other thing I've noticed with these devices is I struggle to get anything other than a SanDisk micro SD card to work. Don't know why. The ones I have are 1TB SanDisk Ultra, but I've also tried every other size below that with success. If you haven't tried one I highly recommend giving the SanDisk SD cards a go. Thank you for your interest! It's a continuous work in progress, learning as I go. (edited)

author: pelgraine
text: If you or anyone else is interested in further testing, here's an alpha of the new 4G (no BLE) standalone version with a couple of notable differences: new Phone app screen with the option of Phone calls for non-contacts or SMS inbox where you can also call saved contacts Dialpad has three method of text entry: pressing the keys directly - so can just press w to get 1, e for 2, etc; or tap the touch screen for the numbers (although this isn't my favourite method as it currently requires tapping pretty exactly on the numbers themselves) or using the old sym+key method standalone IRC app very basic browser search using DuckDuckGo Lite Very basic html web reader browser, still very much in development , best at text-centric websites. Has epub download capability though - if you follow a link to download an epub it will save it to the books folder on your sd card, so you can read it later. Pressing X to clear cookies will also clear your history. Press K to save a bookmark when on a page. To delete bookmarks open the browser home screen, scroll down, and press delete. changed contact saving variables so you now have max 1500 contacts in the standalone variants, and can export them to SD card on the Contacts page - press C from the home screen to open Contacts then X to export, and wait 5-10 seconds (depending on contact numbers) for the popup confirmation - same with R for import contacts IMEI, carrier, and APN details displayed at the bottom of the settings menu, where your node ID and firmware version details are There's a 1400mah version for the stock battery and a 2000mah version if you've put a 2000mah battery in with a custom case (edited)

author: pelgraine
text: Possibly, not sure. I also tend to use VSCode to erase so I can look at the serial output to make sure. But in my experience generally you can often flash a new firmware over the top of the last one without erasing. I've done that with Meshtastic and Launcher and Ripple at times and had them working. With the Meck firmware it's unfortunately not yet possible to send CLI commands to the device over serial. Any changes need to be done on the settings menu in standalone or via the Meshcore Bluetooth app for the BLE firmware variant

text: The way I normally use it with Launcher is just press the reset button > tap Launcher on the launcher boot splash screen on the TD Pro (so that it doesn't auto return to the Meck firmware) > navigate to the WUI button on the Launcher home screen and connect to WiFi > connect to the IP address it gives you on a browser on your computer and use that to upload a copy of the new firmware via the browser to your SD card > press select to disconnect the WUI and navigate back to the SD menu in Launcher > install new firmware (edited)

author: pelgraine
text: The red LED just means your 4G modem is on. It should turn off if you go to settings and change 4G modem to off. As far as I'm aware there's no way to disable it and have 4G connectivity on at the same time. So it looks like your GPS is working if you're getting 12 sentences per second and accumulated 7033. It may just be your area - if I'm in the CBD at ground level I can't get a fix at all, for example, and if I leave GPS on while indoors it does the same thing - just accumulates sentences without getting a fix. Have you taken it outside with clear view to the sky (and away from high-rise building obstructions if you can)? If you have, and it's still not getting a fix outdoors, I'm afraid I'm baffled and confused why it would work with Ripple and not Meck

author: pelgraine

text: Success. GPS is now working, thank you. Here’s what I did. Because the 4g red LED light was always on in R since the update and there is no modem toggle in R (it’s not available) I wondered which settings are stored and where or had become corrupted. The M5 docs say that is on the device. I started again with a fresh SD card and copied fresh Meck 1 4g only to it. Booted with M5, all my previous settings had been saved so they had been on the device. Turned on GPS, connected 12 satellites in two minutes on a window sill. Great, 4g and GPS are fine. Contacts since the latest updates are now a problem across four different devices that I have. Adding and editing has changed or is not possible. I’ll keep going through the menus to see what I’m missing and stay with just one OS on the Pro for now. Many thanks.

author: pelgraine
text: For you and everyone else who wants to test out Meck, the current (temporary) fix for the flasher issue is to select Ripple firmware on the flasher first and do an erase flash - e.g select Ripple GUI: data on SD card (T-Deck Pro v1.1), tick erase device, and flash - and then go back to the main flasher page and flash again via the Custom Firmware option with unmerged Meck v1.1 firmware and don't do the erase this time (edited)

author: pelgraine
text: Ripple just ignores the 4g modem as far as I can tell, as it's not built into the firmware, unfortunately. Hopefully the release of the T-Deck Pro Max will encourage Scott to incorporate it into Ripple! But you're right with Meck - hibernate actually does switch off the radio, WiFi/bluetooth etc so it's all shut down and you need to press the reboot button to turn things on again, as you've noticed. I've also just noticed the hibernate function isn't turning off the 4G modem as well as it should, will look into that. Switching the 4G modem off in the Settings menu when you're not expecting to receive or make calls etc should help with battery life otherwise. Same with turning off the WiFi modem in settings (or Bluetooth from the home page if you've got that version). Or just using hibernate as you have done. GPS uses relatively minimal power so it doesn't seem to make a huge amount of difference whether you keep that on or not. The other thing to extend battery life a little bit is changing your tx power to lower than 22. I haven't done the exact maths but it does help a little if you drop the tx power down to 20 or even 18, in terms of battery life. Other than that, I'm hoping to eventually be able to fine-tune the CPU further so it conserves power without compromising performance - I completely agree that it would be great to have a device like this as a proper phone alternative. Adding/editing contacts is something I'm working on at the moment, similar to the Discover page in Ripple where you see a list of last heard adverts and can add or remove contacts from there. It'll be out in Meck v1.2 once I've ironed out a few more bugs. And you're welcome! It's a pleasure to work on as a fun project (edited)

I realized that it wasn't frozen because it still updated the screen when I got a new message, and then I realized the boot button navigated between the screens and I was able to turn on BLE and GPS and use it companion mode and that worked very well. Eventually the device locked and when I unlocked it everything was working again. I don't have an SD card installed, maybe that would change things. Overall, I like it a lot, the ability to be a companion or not is excellent, I need to try to get the cellular working, overall I'm a really big fan. Can't wait to find out whats next

author: pelgraine
text: Screenshot of a 4 second audio being received. The text message notification gets sent first to the contact to tell the receiver the custom audio type is on the way, then the audio packets (Direct Raw_Custom) in max 158 byte increments, so that one was a little over - 158, 158, 158, 158, 14 - plus the 38 byte pre-text, means 684 bytes in total for that 4 sec audio. I could probably squash it down further than that, but with 1200bps encoding the quality is reasonably good

author: pelgraine
text: For those interested, there is now prerelease remote repeater firmware for both the T-Deck Pro 4G and wifi versions, as well as the T5S3 Pro wifi available for testing. Please note for the wifi remote repeater environments you need to set up the /remote folder in your SD card first and include both your pre-configured mqtt.cfg and wifi.cfg files in that folder prior to flashing. (The 4G repeater only needs the mqtt.cfg file in the /remote folder.) https://github.com/pelgraine/Meck/releases/tag/test Instructions on how to set it up are available in the readme for the 4G remote repeater - https://github.com/pelgraine/Meck/blob/main/README.md I'll include the wifi version instructions later but all you need to do is create a text file, put the name of the wifi network on the first line, the password on the second line, and save it as wifi.cfg. If you have Bmorcelli's Launcher firmware already installed on your device it means you can swap back and forth between repeater and companion firmware as you please. (edited)

author: pelgraine
text: I asked Claude AI to develop a way to send message sans phone app with the Heltec Meshpocket and now we've got morse code messaging with the one user button. 100% vibe coded, made just for fun and because I can. Comes with channel picker pre-selection (channels obviously need to be set beforehand with the companion app, otherwise you're restricted to Public.) Can see incoming messages per channel on the morse screen. Takes a bit to get the hang of the correct timing (test is definitely the easiest word to type!) If anyone wants to have a go, it's the Meshpocket companion BLE build with max 500 contacts, max 8 channels, and 256kb offline queue set, optimised for use with 62.5khz band settings. Code and guide on how to type found here: https://github.com/pelgraine/MeshCore/blob/meshpocket-morse-port/variants/mesh_pocket/Morse_Compose_Guide.md

text: Got my esp32-c6 device today. I have flashed the firmware and confirmed I can access the UI. I am out of time this weekend but want to ask a few questions before I go to test it this week or next weekend. For the updating process - You upload the zip file to the esp through the wifi UI. I THINK (please confirm) the right process on the repeater is to go ahead and put it into DFU mode by sending the "start ota" command on the command line. I then fly the drone with the ESP32 device attached out to the repeater. Stay near enough for it to connect, transfer files and finish the update? If that is all correct, how do I know its done? Just wait with the drone for a few minutes and let things happen? Check the logs after to confirm?

author: cra0
text: hey @mibzzer15 thanks for the questions. Yes I will write up a proper guide for this but this will have to do for now since I'm short on time: 1. When the esp32 starts up for the first time it checks if it has a valid package, if not it starts in AP (Access Point) mode where there is a WI-FI access point for you to connect to (for now called drone-updater with meshcore123 as the password). 2. You connect to that AP and go to the url http://192.168.4.1/ui and upload your zip package for the target meshcore node you want to flash. 3. You then can press reboot to bluetooth or powercycle the esp32 it will then start its scanning constantly. You can pause the scan at any time by pressing the program/boot button. Holding down that button for 5 seconds will force it back into AP mode. 4. You then yes login to the repeaters admin panel and go into the console and write start ota it will then give you the MAC address of the bluetooth advert. 5. You then fly the drone or bring the device close to the repeater (if you have an external antenna it can work up to 10 meters) 6. Typically the update takes 10 seconds but you can wait as long as you like. Right now there is no way to tell what happened this is a problem for later for me to solve 7. If you then try to login again to the device on the meshcore app and it worked and the clock was reset you can tell it worked. 8. To confirm it worked you type ver in the console 9. Then when you get the drone back you can always put it back into AP mode by holding the program/boot button for 5secs then view the logs to see what happened. I hope this helps

author: Thomas
text: I have a Waveshare SX1262 Pico hat, which works well. For possible use as a battery-powered repeater, I looked into power saving and found I could reduce the current by 3 x (32 mA down to 9.5 mA), with some fairly straightforward changes. Before I start trying to write a PR - is there any ongoing discussion about this sort of thing elsewhere? I think the ESP32 variants have a big power saving in the nightlies. Is there interest in implementing these in the codebase? I think these changes are pretty safe and uncontroversial, but there may be some other experience or opinion to consider. Details of the savings: Receive: at 5 volts Vsys, the Pico current consumption is: 32 mA - Release firmware 13.0 mA - Reduce clocks to 18 MHz (48 MHz if USB is connected), enable PFM mode of the Pico SMPS. 12.5 mA - Added __asm volatile ("wfi"); to the end of Dispatcher::loop(). This halts the CPU core every millisecond until the next SysTick interrupt. Needs to be done carefully to avoid breaking other architectures. 11.2 mA - Disable the USB PLL entirely and route the system clock to the ADC peripheral 10.7 mA - Add -D SX126X_REGULATOR=RADIOLIB_SX126X_REGULATOR_DCDC (requires the inductor to be fitted for the built-in buck converter, which it is on this board) 9.5 mA - physically removed the super bright red Power On LED Transmit current drops from 99 mA to 85 mA - for this repeater at home so far, transmit time is only 30 minutes per day, so on average less than 2 mA extra. These are easy firmware changes (apart from the LED), and bring this board down to compete with the lowest power repeaters out there.

author: hecatae
text: I should really look at my other RP2040 boards and see if I can get similar improvements

author: Thomas
text: What boards do you have? I'm happy to share the changes on my github or just paste them here... 95% of the benefit is from the clocks and PLLs which is all done in one file.

author: Thomas
text: here's the PR: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1539 The 9.5 mA is measured at Vsys = 5 V. On a 1s battery it will be closer to 13 or 14 mA.

author: Thomas
text: Hmm, 8 seconds is a long time to stay alive and then crash. It's probably wired correctly otherwise it wouldn't work at all. Power problems? The SX1262 takes 150 mA, a 100 uF capacitor might help? Does the RP2040-zero have the same amount of flash memory as the regular Pico? Could the FW be writing to the filesystem and crashing when it runs out? Wire integrity? Breadboard wires are OK but for high speed SPI the slightest scratch or crackle might crash it. Try wiggling all the breadboard wires gently during the 8 seconds, see if it crashes sooner. On a breadboard there's a small chance of interference between the RF and the jumper wires. Try terminating the antenna connector with a load, or a long cable to the antenna, instead of using a short antenna right next to the board? If you don't have either of those, just hold the antenna firmly in your hand, and press your hand against the grounded case of the RA62, to reduce the radiated RF, and see if that changes anything. OR put the antenna right next to all of the breadboard wires, see if that makes it worse. Keep the jumpers short. Connect several ground wires, not just one.

author: Barry KO6JFI
text: It seems like a denial-of-service attack is possible by sending out flood messages to random addresses, forcing repeaters to use up bandwidth trying to find a path to a client that doesn't exist. Are repeaters set up to throttle these kinds of DOS attacks?

author: KA5PLE (Andy)
text: That is actually a real concern here as well. At the very least a way for repeater operators to ban a certain node would be great to have. And at the same time a way to send a message to the repeater operators of distant repeaters so that we can limit the damage a real attack would do.

author: Barry KO6JFI
text: The sender could randomize their address for every packet. I think it would be very difficult to block this sort of attack.

author: RQ (darco)
text: Yes, it is hard to prevent. One way to possibly mitigate would be for repeaters to realize when they are the first hop of such an attack and start to automatically throttle themselves so that it doesn't take down the whole mesh.

author: retromesh
text: It's only a matter of time unfortunately. I'm not sure there's enough compute power in these devices to have an effective security layer. I think the only chance is first packet validation (a crude implementation of zero trust, if you will) - but how is the question.

author: Neil
text: Difficult to do first-hop avoidance with limited memory, state that you keep about one node possibly leads to the eviction of state that you keep about another

author: NotLebedev
text: Some boards like lilygo t3s3 have slots for SD cards. This enables significantly larger than built in flash memory storage (practically limited by 4GB filesize limit of fat32). This opens possibility to have significantly larger limits for contacts, channels and offline queue as well as some new features like storing all recieved messages on sd card to make them available from any device. I started tinkering with meshcore firmware and identified areas that need to be worked on: 1. Hardware support. Some initialization and configuration code needs to be added to existing board firmwares. I have a working change for t3s3 that enables sd card as secondary fs https://github.com/NotLebedev/MeshCore/tree/t3s3-sd-card . It successfully writes contacts and channels to sd card, but otherwise logic is unchanged 2. Extending contacts, channels and offline queue to support filesystem storage. Currently each of this components resides entirely in memory in some pretty large buffers. Reworking them to support storing them on sd card or partially in memory/partially on sd card will actually allow to increase limits without being stopped by memory consumption. 3. Graceful support for sd cards being changed/removed. Lastly there needs to be some logic to support migration to/from sd card. Currently DataStore::migrateToSecondaryFS only supports migration to empty secondary fs. To support sd cards better some form of merging when sd card is already initialized needs to be added I intend to continue work on this proposal and would appreciate some guidance/feedback

author: Just Stuff™
text: Companion WiFi/BLE/USB is now unified in one Heltec v3 firmware, Testing on V2 (Feel Free To Fork and Open a PR against this branch to add more supported boards) you can switch transport modes at runtime with no reflashing. You can also change WiFi settings live ( wifi.mode , wifi.ssid , wifi.pwd , wifi.ap.ssid , wifi.ap.pwd ) and they persist across reboot. ). https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1968 (edited)

author: Just Stuff™
text: 3.2 meshcli interactive mode (recommended for manual testing) Start interactive mode: meshcore-cli -s /dev/ttyUSB0 -b 115200 Example interactive session: KD3CGK|* set wifi.mode client Var wifi.mode set to client KD3CGK|* get wifi.mode client KD3CGK|* set transport ble Var transport set to ble KD3CGK|* get transport ble KD3CGK|* get wifi.ssid SampleSSID KD3CGK|* get wifi.ap.ssid MeshCore-SampleAP KD3CGK|* get wifi.pwd SamplePwd123 KD3CGK|* get wifi.ap.pwd SampleAPPwd123

author: Just Stuff™
text: Build And Flash This Fork (Heltec V3) If you want to build and flash this fork directly from terminal: git clone https://github.com/just-stuff-tm/MeshCore.git cd MeshCore git checkout feat/heltec-v3-runtime-transport-dev Install PlatformIO Core (if not already installed): python3 -m pip install -U platformio Build firmware: ~/.platformio/penv/bin/pio run -e Heltec_v3_companion_radio_tcp_usb_ble Flash firmware (USB serial example): ~/.platformio/penv/bin/pio run -e Heltec_v3_companion_radio_tcp_usb_ble -t upload --upload-port /dev/ttyUSB0 Optional serial companion check: meshcli -s /dev/ttyUSB0 (edited)

author: Just Stuff™
text: Press Here To Try It Out

author: Just Stuff™
text: Set Commands In meshcli set transport ble set wifi.mode ap set wifi.ap.ssid MeshCore-SampleAP set wifi.ap.pwd SampleAPPwd123 set wifi.mode client set wifi.ssid SampleSSID set wifi.pwd SamplePwd123 The Next 2 enable the exact same thing set transport tcp set transport wifi set tcp.port 5000 --- Get Commands get transport get wifi.mode get wifi.ap.ssid get wifi.ap.pwd get wifi.ssid get wifi.pwd get tcp.port (edited)

author: Just Stuff™
text: “Thanks, that’s helpful. I don’t have a Heltec V2 locally, so I fixed the issues I could verify from the code and from builds, but I still need your retest on actual V2 hardware. I’ve already adjusted the V2 transport build limits to more usable values: MAX_CONTACTS=70 MAX_GROUP_CHANNELS=8 OFFLINE_QUEUE_SIZE=200 I also fixed the false Connected state in the all-transports UI. That was a real bug in the transport connection reporting, not expected behavior. For the WiFi modes: client is the same as the old dedicated WiFi build, where the node joins an existing WiFi network. ap is the new hotspot mode, where the node creates its own WiFi network for direct connection. For transport switching, USB should still be usable in the all-transports build even when BLE is the selected wireless mode. set transport ble/tcp/wifi only changes the wireless side. A normal serial terminal won’t work for the framed companion protocol unless you use meshcli I fixed the code paths for the reported issues, but since I don’t have a V2 on hand, I’d appreciate a retest on your side for: 1. false Connected state 2. long-press/triple-click transport switching 3. usable contact/channel/offline queue sizing 4. USB access via meshcli ” I'm still testing these changes on v3 (edited)

author: Just Stuff™
text: Also to clarify a couple of points from your notes: Double press is not intended to switch transports. On the single-button UI the intended mapping is: single click = next page double click = previous page long press on BLE page = switch wireless transport to WiFi/TCP long press on WiFi page = toggle AP/client triple click on WiFi page = switch wireless transport back to BLE USB is not a separate mode in this build. USB should remain available as the wired companion interface all the time, while set transport ble/tcp/wifi only changes the wireless side. A normal serial terminal like CoolTerm will not talk to the runtime USB interface correctly, because the default USB interface is the framed companion protocol, not the raw text CLI. For normal USB testing you need meshcli or another client that speaks the companion protocol. The raw text CLI is only available through CLI Rescue during boot. So if you retest after the next build, the main extra thing I’m interested in is whether V2 is still unexpectedly rebooting on double-click, since that is not supposed to happen.

author: skykingisepic
text: I have cloned the MeshCore repo Modified the LilyGo Supreme platform.ini and created a wifi section Built and flashed and it's working. Now for the ESP version of the SenseCAP Solar: Under Variants I renamed the sensecap_solar folder to sensecap_solar_nrf and added NRF to each env: section in platform.ini so there are unique build names for NRF and ESP variants. Then I copied the sensecap_solar_nrf folder to a sensecap_solar_esp folder In sensecap_solar_esp/platform.ini I replaced NRF with ESP in each env: section Now I need to replace the NRF parts with ESP parts in platform.ini and make some changes in the SenseCapSolarBoard.cpp file (and others?) since it refers to BLE This is the point where I'm going to have to ask for some guidance or help from those much more experienced to finish this since compiling FW for LoRa boards is all new to me. Hopefully I've laid the groundwork for the two variants and just need to finish the ESP part. I think it will be a quick task and we'll have a new variant to support the SenseCAP Solar P1 with ESP32S3 for WiFi access to MQTT. (edited)

author: Chris
text: re: power, my observed draw for a xiao nrf52 repeater in the field averaged 10mAh with the meshcore firmware, done by measuring how many mAh it took to get the batteries that powered it for two weeks back to full (no solar, just 4 2500mAh 18650s, in parallel, attached to the battery pads on the underside of the repeater). And the solar version using this tiny mppt board shows 8mA at night with the 1.14 firmware (powersaving enabled), from the INA219 built into the board

author: Skye
text: Some local hams asked if they could control their RS232 VHF receiver controllers with a MeshCore repeater remotely, sounds like a perfect use case (low power, RF). Looks like the existing RS232 bridge code depends on Serial in the Arduino library, having a look at the Zephyr docs now looks pretty straightforward. Would just need some new commands to set baud rate, parity, assign pins, etc and a way to send and recieve CRLF strings

author: Skye
text: Even simpler than that, I think these controllers are just a RS232 DCE you send a config string from the MC repeater CLI like send rs232.string "SET TX OFF" , get a string back OK or whatever. Just late night musings ignore me

author: DMan
text: Hmm - the STM32WL has a SubGHz radio inside that just so happens to be compatible with the SX126x. Uses SPI (inside the silicon) and uses all the same registers. I suspect that it's just a Semtec chip packaged next to an STM32 micro lol

author: fri.sch
text: The "sense" is just the same module with additional sensors ( https://wiki.seeedstudio.com/XIAO_BLE/) . But as far as I can see the Zephyr xiao_ble config ( https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/boards/seeed/xiao_ble/doc/index.html) intends to cover exactly that board. So it would be best to base it on that, I think. I will try the current implementation soon and provide feedback, unless someone else is already doing that.

author: fri.sch
text: Ok, that could be the case, yes.
